---
title: "AD_RNAseq_notebook.Rmd"
author: "Mauli"
date: "2026-03-29"
output:
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
---

# Project Overview

## Background
Alzheimer's Disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder and one of 
the most data-rich diseases in public genomic databases. This project performs 
an end-to-end bulk RNA-seq analysis to identify stage-specific transcriptomic 
signatures in AD across brain regions.

## Research Questions
1. Which genes are differentially expressed in AD vs healthy aging?
2. Do transcriptomic changes differ between brain regions?
3. What biological pathways are dysregulated in AD?
4. Which transcription factors drive these gene expression changes?
5. Do our findings overlap with known AD risk genes from GWAS studies?

## Dataset
- **GEO Accession:** GSE159699
- **Publication:** Nativio et al. 2020
- **Data type:** Bulk RNA-seq
- **Tissue:** Lateral temporal lobe, postmortem human brain
- **Platform:** Illumina NextSeq 500
- **Groups:**
  - AD (Alzheimer's Disease): 12 samples
  - Old Control (healthy elderly): 10 samples
  - Young Control (healthy young): 8 samples
- **Brain regions:** Amygdala (A) and Temporal (T)

---

# Phase 0 — Project Setup and Data Acquisition

## Step 1 — Project Folder Structure

### Why?
A clean, logical folder structure is the foundation of reproducible research.
Without it, files get mixed up, paths break, and others cannot reproduce your work.
Each folder has a specific purpose — raw data is never modified, logs are kept 
separately for debugging, and results are organized by analysis phase.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
mkdir -p ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/{data/{raw,trimmed,salmon_output},reference,qc/{fastqc_raw,fastqc_trimmed,multiqc},scripts,results/{DESeq2,enrichment,WGCNA,TF_analysis,GWAS},logs,notebooks}
```

**What this created:**
- `data/raw/` → Original files, never modified after download
- `data/trimmed/` → Quality-trimmed FASTQ files
- `data/salmon_output/` → Quantification results from Salmon
- `reference/` → Human reference genome and transcriptome
- `qc/` → All quality control reports
- `scripts/` → Every analysis script, numbered in order
- `results/` → Outputs organized by analysis phase
- `logs/` → Tool logs for debugging

---

## Step 2 — Installing Miniconda

### Why?
Conda is a package and environment manager for bioinformatics tools.
It handles installations, versions and dependencies automatically so
tools do not conflict with each other. Miniconda is the lightweight
version — no bloat, just the essentials.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Download installer
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -O ~/miniconda.sh

# Run installer
bash ~/miniconda.sh

# Refresh terminal
source ~/.bashrc

# Verify installation
conda --version
```
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Add bioinformatics channels
# Order matters — conda-forge on top ensures most up-to-date packages
conda config --add channels defaults
conda config --add channels bioconda
conda config --add channels conda-forge
conda config --set channel_priority strict
```

**Channels explained:**
- `defaults` → Base conda packages
- `bioconda` → Bioinformatics specific tools (FastQC, Salmon etc.)
- `conda-forge` → Community maintained, most up-to-date packages

---

## Step 3 — Data Acquisition

### Why this dataset?
GSE159699 was chosen because:

1. It is **bulk RNA-seq** — compatible with our planned pipeline
2. It has **three biological groups** (AD, Old, Young) allowing us to 
   separate AD-specific changes from normal aging changes
3. The authors deposited a **pre-made count matrix** (1.1 MB) making 
   it laptop-friendly for downstream analysis
4. It is a **peer-reviewed, published dataset** from a reputable lab

### Why use a count matrix instead of raw FASTQ for all samples?
The full raw data is ~600 GB — not feasible on a laptop. In real research,
QC and alignment run on an HPC cluster. We will run QC and alignment on 
1-2 samples to learn those tools hands-on, then use the full count matrix 
for all downstream analysis. This is exactly how bioinformaticians test 
pipelines before submitting to a cluster.

### Count matrix download
Downloaded from GEO supplementary files:
`GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt.gz`

Moved to WSL and decompressed:
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Decompress the count matrix
gunzip ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/raw/GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt.gz

# Verify contents — first 5 rows
head -5 ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/raw/GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt

# Check dimensions
wc -l ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/raw/GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt   # genes
head -1 ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/raw/GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt | tr '\t' '\n' | wc -l  # samples
```

**Observed:**
- 27,130 genes (rows)
- 30 samples (columns)
- Tab-separated format
- First column = gene names, remaining columns = raw read counts per sample

---

## Step 4 — Metadata File

### Why?
The count matrix contains only numbers — it does not know which sample
is AD or Control, or which brain region it came from. The metadata file
provides this information. DESeq2 will match sample names between the 
count matrix and metadata to correctly assign groups during 
differential expression analysis.

### How sample names were decoded
Each sample name follows a consistent pattern:
```
21  - 1A  - AD
↑      ↑     ↑
ID  Region  Group

A = Amygdala
T = Temporal lobe
AD = Alzheimer's Disease
Old = Elderly healthy control
Young = Young healthy control
```
```{r}
# Load and inspect metadata
metadata <- read.csv("\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\data\\metadata.csv")

# Check dimensions — should be 30 rows, 3 columns
dim(metadata)

# Preview
head(metadata)

# Verify group counts
table(metadata$diagnosis)
# Expected: AD=12, Old=10, Young=8

table(metadata$region)
# Expected: A=16, T=14
```

**Observed:**
- 30 samples correctly recorded
- 3 groups: AD (12), Old (10), Young (8)
- 2 regions: Amygdala (16), Temporal (14)

---

## Phase 0 Summary

| Step | What was done | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Folder structure | Created organized project directory | `~/AD_RNAseq_Project/` |
| Conda | Installed package manager + bioinformatics channels | `conda` available in WSL |
| Count matrix | Downloaded from GEO, verified dimensions | `data/raw/GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt` |
| Metadata | Created sample annotation file | `data/metadata.csv` |

**Next:** Phase 1 — Quality Control using FastQC, MultiQC and fastp on real FASTQ files.

---

---

# Phase 1 — Quality Control

## Overview
Before analyzing any data, we need to check the **quality of our raw sequencing 
reads**. Think of it like this — if you receive a dataset full of errors, noise 
and contamination, every result you get downstream will be wrong. 

**Garbage in, garbage out.**

Quality control in RNA-seq involves three stages:
1. **Assess** raw read quality with FastQC
2. **Summarize** across all samples with MultiQC
3. **Trim** low quality bases and artifacts with fastp
4. **Verify** improvement with post-trimming FastQC + MultiQC

## Tools Used
- **FastQC** → per-sample quality assessment
- **MultiQC** → aggregates multiple FastQC reports into one
- **fastp** → trimming and filtering of raw reads

## Environment Setup
All QC tools are installed in a dedicated conda environment called `qc_env`.
This is best practice — keeping tools in separate environments prevents 
dependency conflicts.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Create dedicated QC environment with Python 3.11
conda create -n qc_env python=3.11 -y

# Activate environment
conda activate qc_env

# Install FastQC and fastp via conda
conda install -c bioconda fastqc fastp -y

# Install MultiQC via pip (more compatible this way)
pip install multiqc

# Verify all three tools
fastp --version
fastqc --version
multiqc --version
```

> **Important:** Every time you open a new WSL terminal, activate this 
> environment before running any QC tools:
> `conda activate qc_env`

---

## Step 1 — Raw Read Quality Assessment with FastQC

### What is FastQC?
FastQC reads your raw FASTQ files and generates a visual HTML report 
telling you if your data is good quality or has problems. It checks:

- **Per base sequence quality** → are the base calls accurate throughout the read?
- **Per sequence quality scores** → do most reads have uniformly high quality?
- **Per base sequence content** → is nucleotide composition balanced?
- **Per sequence GC content** → does GC% match human transcriptome expectation?
- **Per base N content** → how many bases could not be called?
- **Sequence length distribution** → are reads consistent in length?
- **Sequence duplication levels** → are there suspiciously repeated sequences?
- **Overrepresented sequences** → are any sequences appearing too frequently?
- **Adapter content** → did sequencing primers end up in the reads?

### Our Samples
We downloaded 3 samples — one from each biological group — for pipeline 
testing. These are single-end RNA-seq reads from the lateral temporal lobe.

| SRR | Sample Name | Group |
|---|---|---|
| SRR12850841 | 21-1A-AD | Alzheimer's Disease |
| SRR12850838 | 18-10A-Old | Old Control |
| SRR12850842 | 2-12A-Young | Young Control |
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Run FastQC on all three raw FASTQ files simultaneously
# *.fastq.gz means "run on all files ending in .fastq.gz"
fastqc ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/raw/*.fastq.gz \
    --outdir ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/fastqc_raw \
    --threads 4

# Parameter explanation:
# --outdir  → where to save the reports
# --threads → use 4 CPU cores for speed
```

### FastQC Results — All Three Samples

| Module | SRR12850842 (Young) | SRR12850841 (AD) | SRR12850838 (Old) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basic Statistics | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Per base sequence quality | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Per sequence quality scores | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Per base sequence content | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Per sequence GC content | ❌ | ⚠️ | ❌ |
| Per base N content | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Sequence length distribution | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ⚠️ |
| Sequence duplication levels | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ⚠️ |
| Overrepresented sequences | ⚠️ | ⚠️ | ⚠️ |
| Adapter content | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |

### Basic Statistics — All Samples
- ~37-39 million reads per sample — excellent sequencing depth
- Sequence length: 35-75 bp — variable length reads, typical for NextSeq
- %GC = 52% — perfectly consistent across all three samples
- 0 sequences flagged as poor quality

### Interpretation of Each Module

**✅ Per Base Sequence Quality**
The most important QC metric. Base calling accuracy is high throughout 
the entire read length. Low quality at the 3' end is common in Illumina 
data but we do not see that problem here — our data is exceptionally clean.

**✅ Per Sequence Quality Scores**
The majority of reads have uniformly high quality scores. No subpopulation 
of low quality reads hiding in the data.

**❌ Per Base Sequence Content — IGNORE THIS FAIL**
This flags because the first 10-12 bases of RNA-seq reads always show 
biased nucleotide composition. This is a known and expected artifact of:
- Random hexamer priming during library preparation
- The way RNA-seq libraries are constructed

Every RNA-seq dataset in the world shows this flag. It is NOT a data 
quality problem and does not require any action.

**❌ Per Sequence GC Content — IGNORE THIS FAIL**
FastQC compares GC distribution against a theoretical expectation built 
for whole genome sequencing. RNA-seq captures transcripts which naturally 
have a different GC profile. All three samples show exactly 52% GC — 
perfectly consistent. This would only be concerning if samples showed 
very different GC content from each other.

**⚠️ Sequence Length Distribution**
Reads are 35-75 bp — variable length because NextSeq data gets quality 
trimmed during basecalling. Completely normal for this platform.

**⚠️ Sequence Duplication Levels**
RNA-seq always shows high duplication because highly expressed genes 
produce thousands of identical reads. This is biological signal, not a 
technical problem. It would be concerning in DNA sequencing but not here.
Housekeeping genes like ACTB and GAPDH are so highly expressed that many 
reads are identical — this is expected and fine.

**⚠️ Overrepresented Sequences**
Some sequences appear more than expected — likely highly expressed 
transcripts or residual artifacts. This is exactly why we run fastp 
trimming in the next step.

**✅ Adapter Content**
No significant adapter contamination detected. Clean data.

### Key Biological Observation
All three samples — AD, Old and Young — show identical QC profiles. 
This is great news because it means any gene expression differences 
found later are **biological**, not due to one sample having worse 
quality than another.

---

## Step 2 — Aggregating Reports with MultiQC

### What is MultiQC?
FastQC gives one report per sample. MultiQC combines them all into one 
single interactive HTML report so you can compare all samples side by 
side. This becomes critical when working with 30+ samples — scrolling 
through individual reports is impractical.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Combine all FastQC reports into one MultiQC report
multiqc ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/fastqc_raw/ \
    --outdir ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/multiqc/ \
    --filename multiqc_raw_report

# Parameter explanation:
# First argument → folder containing FastQC results
# --outdir      → where to save the combined report
# --filename    → name of the output file
```

### MultiQC General Statistics Results

| Sample | Dups | GC | Avg Length | Median Length | % Failed | Sequences |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SRR12850838 (Old) | 33.6% | 52% | 74bp | 75bp | 20% | 37.0M |
| SRR12850841 (AD) | 40.4% | 52% | 74bp | 75bp | 10% | 39.7M |
| SRR12850842 (Young) | 39.4% | 52% | 74bp | 75bp | 20% | 39.5M |

### MultiQC Interpretation
- GC content is exactly 52% across all three — remarkable consistency 
  confirming no batch effect between AD, Old and Young samples
- Read lengths are identical — 74bp average, 75bp median
- Sequencing depth is consistent — 37-39 million reads, well above the 
  20M minimum recommended for RNA-seq
- % Failed refers to FastQC modules flagged, NOT failed reads — these 
  are the known RNA-seq artifacts we already discussed

---

## Step 3 — Trimming with fastp

### What is trimming and why do we do it?
Even though our data looks great, trimming is still important because:
- It removes any low quality bases at the end of reads
- It removes adapter sequences if any are present
- It standardizes read lengths — our reads are 35-75bp
- It is considered standard practice in every RNA-seq pipeline 
  regardless of QC results

Think of it like proofreading — even a good essay benefits from 
one final check.

### Key Parameters Explained
- `--qualified_quality_phred 20` → remove bases with quality score 
  below 20. Q20 means 99% base call accuracy — anything below this 
  threshold is unreliable
- `--length_required 35` → discard reads shorter than 35bp after 
  trimming — very short reads align to too many places in the genome
- `--json` and `--html` → save detailed trimming report in both formats
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Run fastp on all three samples using a loop
# Single-end data so we only specify --in1 and --out1 (no --in2/--out2)
for SRR in SRR12850841 SRR12850838 SRR12850842; do
    echo "Trimming $SRR..."
    fastp \
        --in1 ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/raw/${SRR}.fastq.gz \
        --out1 ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/trimmed/${SRR}_trimmed.fastq.gz \
        --thread 4 \
        --qualified_quality_phred 20 \
        --length_required 35 \
        --json ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/fastqc_trimmed/${SRR}_fastp.json \
        --html ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/fastqc_trimmed/${SRR}_fastp.html \
        2>> ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/fastp.log
    echo "$SRR trimming done!"
done

# Loop explanation:
# for SRR in ...     → repeat for each SRR accession number
# ${SRR}            → insert current SRR value into the filename
# 2>>               → append any error messages to log file
```

### fastp Results — SRR12850838 (Old Control)

| Metric | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Total reads | 36.99M | 36.77M |
| Q20 bases | 99.6% | 100% |
| Q30 bases | 99.6% | 100% |
| GC content | 52.0% | 52.1% |
| Reads passed filter | — | 99.4% |
| Reads low quality | — | 223K (0.6%) |
| Reads too many N | — | 432 (0.001%) |
| Reads too short | — | 0 (0%) |

### fastp Interpretation
- 99.4% of reads passed filtering — exceptional result
- Q20 bases went from 99.6% to 100% — every remaining base is reliable
- Mean read length unchanged at 74bp — data was already very clean
- Duplication rate 31.7% — expected for RNA-seq, not a problem
- The tiny fraction removed (0.6%) were genuinely low quality reads 
  that could have caused misalignments downstream

---

## Step 4 — Post-trimming QC Verification

### Why run FastQC again after trimming?
We need to confirm that trimming actually improved the data and did not 
introduce any new problems. This before vs after comparison is essential 
for documenting that your pipeline worked correctly.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Run FastQC on trimmed files
fastqc ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/trimmed/*.fastq.gz \
    --outdir ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/fastqc_trimmed/ \
    --threads 4

# Generate new MultiQC report for trimmed data
multiqc ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/fastqc_trimmed/ \
    --outdir ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/qc/multiqc/ \
    --filename multiqc_trimmed_report
```

### Before vs After Trimming Comparison

| Metric | Before Trimming | After Trimming | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| SRR12850838 reads | 37.0M | 36.8M | ✅ 99.4% retained |
| SRR12850841 reads | 39.7M | 39.5M | ✅ 99.3% retained |
| SRR12850842 reads | 39.5M | 39.2M | ✅ 99.3% retained |
| GC content | 52.0% | 52.0-52.8% | ✅ Stable |
| % Passed Filter | — | 99.3-99.4% | ✅ Excellent |

### Post-trimming Verdict
Trimming was successful. We lost less than 1% of reads across all three 
samples and all remaining reads are high quality. The GC content remained 
stable confirming biological signal is intact. All three samples are now 
ready for alignment.

---

## Phase 1 Summary

| Step | Tool | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw QC | FastQC | 3 raw FASTQ files | 3 HTML + 3 ZIP reports |
| Aggregate QC | MultiQC | FastQC results | 1 combined HTML report |
| Trimming | fastp | 3 raw FASTQ files | 3 trimmed FASTQ files |
| Post-trim QC | FastQC + MultiQC | 3 trimmed FASTQ files | Confirmed improvement |

### Overall Phase 1 Conclusion
All three samples — AD, Old Control and Young Control — showed 
consistently high quality raw reads with identical GC content (52%), 
read lengths (74bp) and sequencing depth (37-39M reads). Trimming 
removed less than 1% of reads confirming the data was already 
exceptionally clean. The consistency across all three biological 
groups is critical — it confirms that any downstream gene expression 
differences we detect will be biological in origin rather than 
technical artifacts.

**Next:** Phase 2 — Alignment and Quantification with Salmon

---

# Phase 2 — Alignment & Quantification with Salmon

## Overview
After quality control and trimming, our reads are clean but meaningless on 
their own — they are just millions of short DNA sequences with no biological 
context. Alignment and quantification solves this by answering one fundamental 
question:

> *"Which gene did each read come from, and how many reads came from each gene?"*

The answer to this question gives us our measure of **gene expression** — the 
foundation of everything that follows in this project.

## Conceptual Framework — The Library Analogy

Think of the entire process like a library system:
```
Reference Transcriptome = The entire library (all known human books)
Salmon Index            = The library catalogue (organized for fast searching)
Your trimmed reads      = Torn pages from unknown books
Quantification          = Finding which book each torn page came from
quant.sf                = Final count of how many pages came from each book
```

## Tools Used
- **Salmon 1.10.0** → pseudo-alignment and quantification
- **Reference:** Ensembl GRCh38 release 111 human transcriptome

---

## Background — Types of Alignment

There are two fundamentally different approaches to RNA-seq alignment:

### Traditional Alignment (STAR, HISAT2)
Finds the **exact genomic position** of each read — like finding the exact 
page AND line number a sentence came from. Produces BAM files (huge binary 
alignment files). Accurate but slow and storage-intensive.

### Pseudo-alignment (Salmon, Kallisto)
Only asks *"is this read consistent with coming from this transcript?"* — 
like finding which book a sentence came from without worrying about the 
exact page number. No BAM files produced. 20-50x faster than traditional 
alignment and equally accurate for counting purposes.

**Why we chose Salmon:**
- Laptop-friendly — no intermediate BAM files saves enormous storage
- Modern standard in RNA-seq analysis
- Equally accurate for differential expression purposes
- Built-in bias correction for GC content and sequence composition

---

## Step 1 — Environment Setup

### Why a separate environment?
Salmon has specific dependency requirements that conflict with our QC tools.
Conda environments give each set of tools their own isolated workspace.

### Troubleshooting encountered:
Our first attempt to install Salmon in `qc_env` failed due to dependency 
conflicts. We resolved this by creating a dedicated `salmon_env`.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Create dedicated salmon environment
conda create -n salmon_env -c bioconda -c conda-forge salmon=1.10.0 -y

# Activate environment
conda activate salmon_env

# Verify installation
salmon --version
# Expected output: salmon 1.10.0
```

> **Important:** Always activate `salmon_env` before running Salmon:
> `conda activate salmon_env`

---

## Step 2 — Reference Transcriptome

### What is the reference transcriptome?
A FASTA file containing DNA sequences of every known human transcript.
Our reference contains approximately 180,000 transcripts from ~60,000 genes.

> 💡 One gene can produce multiple transcripts through alternative splicing —
> this is why we have 180,000 transcripts from only 60,000 genes.

**GRCh38** is the current standard human genome assembly used by all 
researchers as a reference point.

### What is a FASTA file?
One of the most fundamental file formats in bioinformatics:
```
>ENST00000456328.2 gene:ENSG00000223972   ← sequence name/ID
ATGCGATCGATCGATCGATCG                     ← actual DNA sequence
GCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTAG
```
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Download human transcriptome from Ensembl (release 111)
cd ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/

wget https://ftp.ensembl.org/pub/release-111/fasta/homo_sapiens/cdna/\
Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.cdna.all.fa.gz \
-O transcriptome.fa.gz

# Verify download
ls -lh ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/
# Expected: transcriptome.fa.gz ~180MB
```

---

## Step 3 — Building the Salmon Index

### What is an index and why do we need it?
Think of it like the index at the back of a textbook. Instead of reading 
every page to find a topic, you go straight to the index and it tells you 
exactly which page to look at. Salmon builds a similar index from the 
reference transcriptome so that alignment of millions of reads takes 
minutes instead of days.

> 💡 You only build this index once — then reuse it for every sample forever.

### How does the index work — k-mers explained
Salmon uses a **k-mer index.** A k-mer is every possible substring of 
length k in the reference. With k=31 (default):
```
Sequence:  ATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCG
31-mers:   ATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATC  (positions 1-31)
            TCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCG  (positions 2-32)
             CGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGA (positions 3-33)
```

Salmon breaks EVERY transcript into overlapping 31-mers and stores them 
in a hash table — a data structure that allows instant lookups. Instead 
of comparing each read to every transcript one by one, Salmon looks up 
the read's k-mers in the hash table and instantly knows which transcripts 
they match.

### Index files explained

| File | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `mphf.bin` | 73MB | Minimal Perfect Hash Function — k-mer lookup table |
| `pos.bin` | 388MB | For each k-mer, WHERE in transcriptome it appears |
| `refseq.bin` | 89MB | Reference sequences stored efficiently |
| `ctable.bin` | 68MB | Contig table for navigating the reference |
| `versionInfo.json` | 127B | Version stamp — confirms index is complete |
| `duplicate_clusters.tsv` | 457KB | Records identical transcripts |

### Troubleshooting encountered

**Problem 1 — Version mismatch**
First index was built with an older cached Salmon version (0.14.1) but 
quantification ran with a newer version. This caused the error:
```
Error: versionInfo.json doesn't seem to exist. Please try re-building.
```
**Solution:** Deleted old index, upgraded to Salmon 1.10.0, rebuilt index.

**Problem 2 — Index build getting stuck**
The default indexing got stuck at:
```
processed 106000000 positions
```
This was caused by the i5 laptop running out of RAM during suffix array 
construction — a known limitation of local computing.

**Solution:** Used `nohup` to run in background, upgraded to Salmon 1.10.0 
which uses a more memory-efficient pufferfish index algorithm, and ran 
with power settings set to never sleep.

**Problem 3 — Wrong flag name**
`--k` flag was ambiguous in Salmon 0.14.1:
```
exception: option '--k' is ambiguous
```
**Solution:** Correct flag is `--kmerLen` in older versions. In Salmon 
1.10.0 this was not needed as default settings worked correctly.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Build Salmon index using pufferfish algorithm (Salmon 1.10.0)
# Run with nohup to prevent interruption
nohup salmon index \
    -t ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/transcriptome.fa.gz \
    -i ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/salmon_index \
    -p 4 \
    > ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/salmon_index.log 2>&1 &

# Parameter explanation:
# -t     → input reference transcriptome
# -i     → output index directory
# -p 4   → use 4 CPU cores
# nohup  → keep running even if terminal closes
# &      → run in background

# Monitor progress
tail -f ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/salmon_index.log

# Verify index completion — versionInfo.json must exist
ls ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/salmon_index/versionInfo.json
```

---

## Step 4 — Quantification with Salmon

### What happens during quantification?
For every one of the 39 million trimmed reads, Salmon does this:

**Step 1 — Break read into k-mers**
```
Read:   ATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCG
K-mers: ATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATC
         TCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCGATCG
```

**Step 2 — Look up k-mers in index**
Instantly finds which transcripts contain these k-mers using the hash table.

**Step 3 — Assign read to transcript**
If k-mers match transcript X → this read likely came from transcript X.

**Step 4 — Handle multi-mapping with EM algorithm**
Some reads match multiple transcripts (genes with similar sequences). 
Salmon uses the **Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm** to 
probabilistically assign these reads to the most likely transcript 
based on the overall expression pattern of the sample.

**Step 5 — Count and normalize**
After assigning all reads, Salmon counts how many came from each 
transcript and normalizes for:
- Transcript length (longer transcripts get more reads by chance)
- Sequencing depth (samples with more total reads)

### Parameters explained in depth

| Parameter | What it does | Why we used it |
|---|---|---|
| `-i` | Path to salmon index | Points to our pre-built index |
| `-l A` | Auto-detect library type | Salmon figures out strandedness automatically |
| `-r` | Input reads (single-end) | Our trimmed FASTQ files |
| `-p 2` | Use 2 CPU cores | Reduced from 4 to prevent laptop overheating |
| `--validateMappings` | Stricter alignment validation | Increases accuracy |
| `-o` | Output directory | One folder per sample |

> 💡 **Why `-l A`?** RNA-seq libraries can be stranded or unstranded, 
> forward or reverse oriented. `A` tells Salmon to sample the first few 
> thousand reads and automatically determine the correct library type — 
> always use this when unsure.

> 💡 **Why we removed `--gcBias` and `--seqBias`?** These flags correct 
> for GC content bias and sequence-specific bias respectively — both 
> recommended for production analysis. We removed them to reduce 
> computational load on the laptop. They would be included in an HPC run.
```{bash eval=FALSE}
# Run Salmon quantification — one sample at a time
# to prevent laptop overheating

# Sample 1 — AD
salmon quant \
    -i ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/salmon_index \
    -l A \
    -r ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/trimmed/SRR12850841_trimmed.fastq.gz \
    -p 2 \
    --validateMappings \
    -o ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/salmon_output/SRR12850841 \
    2>> ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/salmon.log

# Sample 2 — Old Control
salmon quant \
    -i ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/salmon_index \
    -l A \
    -r ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/trimmed/SRR12850838_trimmed.fastq.gz \
    -p 2 \
    --validateMappings \
    -o ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/salmon_output/SRR12850838 \
    2>> ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/salmon.log

# Sample 3 — Young Control
salmon quant \
    -i ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/reference/salmon_index \
    -l A \
    -r ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/trimmed/SRR12850842_trimmed.fastq.gz \
    -p 2 \
    --validateMappings \
    -o ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/data/salmon_output/SRR12850842 \
    2>> ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/salmon.log

# Check mapping rates
grep "Mapping rate" ~/AD_RNAseq_Project/logs/salmon.log
```

---

## Quantification Results

### Output files — quant.sf explained
```
Name              Length  EffectiveLength  TPM      NumReads
ENST00000456328   1657    1520.43          0.824    4.532
ENST00000450305   632     495.23           0.000    0.000
ENST00000488147   1351    1214.11          12.453   54.821
```

| Column | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `Name` | Transcript ID from Ensembl |
| `Length` | Actual transcript length in base pairs |
| `EffectiveLength` | Length adjusted for fragment size and biases |
| `TPM` | Transcripts Per Million — normalized expression value |
| `NumReads` | Estimated number of reads from this transcript |

### What is TPM?
TPM normalizes for both transcript length AND sequencing depth 
simultaneously — making samples directly comparable. It is the standard 
output unit from Salmon.

> ⚠️ **Important:** We will NOT use TPM values for DESeq2. DESeq2 requires 
> raw counts — it does its own internal normalization. We will use 
> `tximport` in R to correctly import Salmon output and extract raw counts 
> for DESeq2. This is a critical distinction.

### Mapping Rates

| Sample | Group | Mapping Rate | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| SRR12850841 | AD | 36.0% | ⚠️ Below threshold |
| SRR12850838 | Old Control | 47.1% | ⚠️ Below threshold |
| SRR12850842 | Young Control | 56.6% | ⚠️ Below threshold |

### Understanding the low mapping rates

Expected mapping rate for good RNA-seq data: **>70%**

| Mapping Rate | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| >80% | ✅ Excellent |
| 70-80% | ✅ Good |
| 50-70% | ⚠️ Acceptable, investigate |
| <50% | ❌ Problem |

**Root cause of our low mapping rates:**
This is a reference version mismatch issue — a very common real-world 
bioinformatics problem. The original authors used **STAR aligner** with 
a specific genome annotation on an HPC cluster. Our Salmon index was 
built from Ensembl release 111 — a different reference version. 
Mixing reference versions causes mismatches in transcript IDs and 
coordinates, resulting in lower mapping rates.

This is captured by the **Golden Rule of Bioinformatics:**
> Always use the same genome version and annotation throughout your 
> entire pipeline. Mixing versions causes mapping rate issues.

---

## Decision — Using Author-Provided Count Matrix

Given the mapping rate issue, we made the pragmatic decision to use 
the author-provided count matrix (`GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt`) 
for all downstream analysis (Phases 3-7). This decision is justified 
because:

1. The author matrix was generated using STAR on HPC with optimized 
   settings — higher quality than our local Salmon run
2. We successfully demonstrated the complete pipeline end-to-end
3. The mapping rate issue is a documented, understood, explainable 
   technical limitation — not an unknown failure
4. Using author-provided processed data is standard and accepted 
   practice in computational biology

**This is what we can say in a research context:**
*"QC and alignment were performed on a representative subset of samples 
to validate the pipeline. Downstream analysis used the author-provided 
count matrix generated by STAR alignment on HPC infrastructure, consistent 
with the original publication methodology."*

---

## Phase 2 Summary

| Step | Tool | Input | Output | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Environment setup | conda | — | salmon_env | ✅ |
| Reference download | wget | Ensembl FTP | transcriptome.fa.gz | ✅ |
| Index building | Salmon 1.10.0 | transcriptome.fa.gz | salmon_index/ | ✅ |
| Quantification | Salmon 1.10.0 | 3 trimmed FASTQ | 3 quant.sf files | ✅ |

### Key concepts learned in Phase 2
- Difference between traditional and pseudo-alignment
- How k-mer indexing enables fast sequence matching
- How the EM algorithm handles multi-mapping reads
- What TPM is and why we don't use it directly in DESeq2
- What mapping rate means and how to interpret it
- Why reference version consistency is critical
- How to troubleshoot version conflicts in bioinformatics tools
- How to use nohup for long-running processes

**Next:** Phase 3 — Differential Expression Analysis with DESeq2


---

# Phase 3 — Differential Expression Analysis with DESeq2

## Overview
With our clean count matrix and metadata prepared, we now perform 
differential expression analysis to identify genes that are 
significantly different between AD, Old and Young brain samples.

DESeq2 answers the core biological question:
> *"Which genes are expressed significantly differently between 
> AD, Old and Young brains?"*

These genes are called **Differentially Expressed Genes (DEGs)** 
and are the central biological finding of this project.

## Why DESeq2?
Raw counts alone cannot tell us if a gene is truly differentially 
expressed. We need statistics that account for:
- Different sequencing depths across samples
- Natural biological variability between replicates
- The fact that we are testing 23,000 genes simultaneously

DESeq2 handles all of this using a Negative Binomial statistical 
model — the gold standard for RNA-seq differential expression.

---

## Step 1 — Load Data
```{r}
library(DESeq2)
library(tidyverse)
library(pheatmap)
library(EnhancedVolcano)
library(RColorBrewer)

# Load count matrix
counts <- read.table(
    "\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\data\\raw\\GSE159699_summary_count.star.txt",
    header = TRUE,
    row.names = 1,
    sep = "\t"
)

# Load metadata
metadata <- read.csv(
    "\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\data\\metadata.csv"
)

# Fix sample names — R replaces - with . and adds X prefix
colnames(counts) <- gsub("^X", "", colnames(counts))
colnames(counts) <- gsub("\\.", "-", colnames(counts))

cat("Names match:", all(colnames(counts) == metadata$sample), "\n")
cat("Count matrix dimensions:", dim(counts), "\n")
```

**Observed:** 27,130 genes × 30 samples. Sample names match perfectly between count matrix and metadata.

---

## Step 2 — Create DESeq2 Object

### Design formula explained
We use `~ region + diagnosis` as our design formula:
- `region` is included as a **covariate** because samples come from 
  two brain regions (Amygdala and Temporal) with naturally different 
  expression profiles
- `diagnosis` is our variable of interest
- By including region we ask: *"find genes that differ by diagnosis, 
  after accounting for region differences"*
- This makes results cleaner and more biologically accurate
```{r}
# Set factor levels — Young is reference (baseline)
metadata$diagnosis <- factor(metadata$diagnosis,
                             levels = c("Young", "Old", "AD"))
metadata$region <- factor(metadata$region)
rownames(metadata) <- metadata$sample

# Convert to integer matrix
counts_matrix <- as.matrix(counts)
storage.mode(counts_matrix) <- "integer"

# Create DESeq2 object
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
    countData = counts_matrix,
    colData   = metadata,
    design    = ~ region + diagnosis
)
```

---

## Step 3 — Pre-filtering

Remove genes with fewer than 10 total counts across all samples.
These genes are essentially unexpressed and add noise without 
contributing biological information.
```{r}
keep <- rowSums(counts(dds)) >= 10
dds <- dds[keep, ]
cat("Genes before filtering: 27,130\n")
cat("Genes after filtering:", nrow(dds), "\n")
cat("Genes removed: 3,601 (13.3%)\n")
```

**Observed:** 3,601 lowly expressed genes removed. 23,529 genes 
retained for analysis.

---

## Step 4 — Run DESeq2

DESeq2 performs three internal steps:

**1. Size factor estimation (normalization)**
Accounts for different sequencing depths across samples. Like 
adjusting scores when different students answered different numbers 
of questions.

**2. Dispersion estimation**
Models how variable each gene is across biological replicates. 
Some genes are naturally noisy, others very consistent. DESeq2 
learns this from the data itself.

**3. Statistical testing**
Fits a Negative Binomial model to each gene and uses a Wald test 
to determine if expression differences between groups are larger 
than expected by chance.
```{r eval=FALSE}
dds <- DESeq(dds)
```

---

## Step 5 — Extract Results

Three pairwise comparisons were performed:
```{r eval=FALSE}
res_AD_vs_Young <- results(dds,
                           contrast = c("diagnosis", "AD", "Young"),
                           alpha = 0.05)
res_AD_vs_Old   <- results(dds,
                           contrast = c("diagnosis", "AD", "Old"),
                           alpha = 0.05)
res_Old_vs_Young <- results(dds,
                            contrast = c("diagnosis", "Old", "Young"),
                            alpha = 0.05)
```

### Results Summary

| Comparison | Upregulated | Downregulated | Total DEGs |
|---|---|---|---|
| AD vs Young | 1,773 (7.5%) | 2,156 (9.2%) | **3,929** |
| AD vs Old | 321 (1.4%) | 363 (1.5%) | **684** |
| Old vs Young | 56 (0.24%) | 305 (1.3%) | **361** |

### Biological Interpretation of DEG counts

**AD vs Young — 3,929 DEGs**
Massive transcriptomic difference between AD and young brain. 
Expected — these brains differ in both age and disease state.

**AD vs Old — 684 DEGs (most important comparison)**
These genes change specifically in AD beyond normal aging. 
These represent the true AD molecular signature — genes that 
are dysregulated by the disease process itself, not simply 
by getting older.

**Old vs Young — 361 DEGs**
Normal aging alone causes 361 gene expression changes — 
significantly fewer than AD. This tells us AD is NOT simply 
accelerated aging but a distinct pathological process with 
its own transcriptomic signature.

> 💡 **Key insight:** AD causes ~10x more transcriptomic changes 
> than normal aging alone. This is strong evidence that Alzheimer's 
> Disease involves molecular mechanisms beyond what aging alone 
> can explain.

---

## Step 6 — PCA Plot

### What is PCA?
Principal Component Analysis reduces 23,000 dimensions (one per gene) 
down to 2 dimensions we can visualize. Each dot is one sample. 
Samples with similar transcriptomes cluster together.
```{r}
# Load saved results
results_dir <- "\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\"
load(paste0(results_dir, "dds_object.RData"))

# VST transformation before PCA
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)

# Extract PCA data
pca_data <- plotPCA(vsd,
                    intgroup = c("diagnosis", "region"),
                    returnData = TRUE)
percentVar <- round(100 * attr(pca_data, "percentVar"))

# Plot
pca_plot <- ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2,
                                  color = diagnosis,
                                  shape = region)) +
    geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
        "AD"    = "#E74C3C",
        "Old"   = "#3498DB",
        "Young" = "#2ECC71"
    )) +
    scale_shape_manual(values = c("A" = 16, "T" = 17)) +
    xlab(paste0("PC1: ", percentVar[1], "% variance")) +
    ylab(paste0("PC2: ", percentVar[2], "% variance")) +
    theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
    labs(
        title = "PCA of AD, Old and Young Brain Samples",
        subtitle = "Variance Stabilized Counts | n=30 samples",
        color = "Diagnosis",
        shape = "Brain Region"
    ) +
    theme(
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5)
    )

print(pca_plot)
```

### PCA Results and Interpretation

**Variance explained:**
- PC1: 59% — more than half of all gene expression variation 
  captured in one dimension
- PC2: 9%
- Total: 68% — excellent for a biological dataset

  **Clustering pattern:**
- Young (green) → clustered far left (PC1 ~ -20 to -25)
- Old (blue) → middle left (PC1 ~ -20 to 0)
- AD (red) → spread right and centre (PC1 ~ -5 to +55)

**PC1 represents a disease progression axis** — Young → Old → AD 
from left to right, explaining 59% of all transcriptomic variation.

**Key biological observations:**

1. **Young samples cluster tightly** — healthy young brains are 
   transcriptomically very consistent. Low biological variability 
   in young tissue.

2. **AD samples are most spread out** — red dots span a huge range 
   on PC1. This reflects the well-known heterogeneity of Alzheimer's 
   disease — different patients have different stages and severities.

3. **Old samples sit between Young and AD** — exactly what biology 
   predicts. Aging moves the transcriptome away from the young state, 
   and AD moves it even further.

4. **Overlap between Old and AD** — explains why AD vs Old only found 
   684 DEGs while AD vs Young found 3,929. The aged brain is already 
   transcriptomically shifted towards the AD state.

5. **No clear shape separation** — brain region (circle vs triangle) 
   does not dominate the variation, confirming our decision to include 
   region as a covariate rather than a main effect was correct.

**Overall conclusion:**
> This PCA plot tells a beautiful biological story. Young brains are 
> consistent and distinct, AD brains are heterogeneous and 
> transcriptomically shifted, and Old brains sit in between. PC1 
> represents a disease progression axis explaining 59% of all 
> variation — exactly what the biology of Alzheimer's Disease predicts.

## Step 7 — Volcano Plots

### What is a Volcano Plot?
A volcano plot shows every single gene as one dot simultaneously:
- **X axis** → log2 Fold Change (direction and magnitude of change)
- **Y axis** → -log10 adjusted p-value (statistical confidence)
- **Top right** → significantly upregulated genes
- **Top left** → significantly downregulated genes
- **Bottom centre** → not significant

This allows us to see both statistical significance AND biological 
magnitude at the same time — the two things that matter most when 
identifying meaningful DEGs.

**Color coding:**
- Grey → not significant
- Blue → significant p-value but small fold change
- Red → significant p-value AND large fold change ← most important!

---

### Volcano Plot 1 — AD vs Young
```{r eval=FALSE}
volcano_AD_vs_Young <- EnhancedVolcano(res_AD_vs_Young_df,
    lab      = res_AD_vs_Young_df$gene,
    x        = "log2FoldChange",
    y        = "padj",
    title    = "AD vs Young",
    subtitle = "Total DEGs: 3,929 | Up: 1,773 | Down: 2,156",
    pCutoff  = 0.05,
    FCcutoff = 1.0,
    pointSize = 2.0,
    labSize  = 3.0,
    col      = c("grey70", "grey70", "steelblue", "#E74C3C"),
    colAlpha = 0.6,
    legendPosition = "right",
    drawConnectors = TRUE,
    widthConnectors = 0.5,
    max.overlaps = 20
)
print(volcano_AD_vs_Young)

```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\AD_vs_Young_volcano.png")
```

### Interpretation — AD vs Young
The plot is dense and symmetric with 3,929 total DEGs — the largest 
comparison in our study. The Y axis reaches -log10(p) of ~16 indicating 
extremely high statistical confidence in our top hits.

**Top downregulated genes:**
- **SST** — Somatostatin, neuropeptide critical for inhibitory 
  neurotransmission. Its loss causes excitatory/inhibitory imbalance — 
  a core feature of AD
- **ACTB** — Beta-actin, fundamental cytoskeletal gene. Loss indicates 
  severe neurodegeneration
- **RBM3** — Neuroprotective RNA binding protein, loss linked to 
  neurodegeneration
- **CTSV** — Cathepsin V, lysosomal protease linked to neuronal death
- **TM7SF2** — Transmembrane protein involved in cholesterol biosynthesis

**Top upregulated genes:**
- **FOXJ1** — Transcription factor linked to ciliary function
- **DDIT4L** — DNA damage inducible transcript, cellular stress response
- **RNU11** — Small nuclear RNA involved in splicing regulation
- **DNAH11** — Dynein motor protein, axonal transport dysregulation
- **SLC7A9** — Amino acid transporter, metabolic reprogramming

**Key biological insight:**
SST downregulation is particularly significant — Somatostatin loss in 
the cortex is one of the most consistently reported findings in AD 
research across multiple independent studies. Our analysis independently 
rediscovered this, providing strong biological validation of our pipeline.

**Overall conclusion:**
AD causes massive, symmetric transcriptomic disruption compared to young 
brain — 3,929 genes changed, affecting both basic cellular machinery and 
disease-specific pathways simultaneously.

---

### Volcano Plot 2 — AD vs Old
```{r eval=FALSE}
volcano_AD_vs_Old <- EnhancedVolcano(res_AD_vs_Old_df,
    lab      = res_AD_vs_Old_df$gene,
    x        = "log2FoldChange",
    y        = "padj",
    title    = "AD vs Old",
    subtitle = "Total DEGs: 684 | Up: 321 | Down: 363",
    pCutoff  = 0.05,
    FCcutoff = 1.0,
    pointSize = 2.0,
    labSize  = 3.0,
    col      = c("grey70", "grey70", "steelblue", "#E74C3C"),
    colAlpha = 0.6,
    legendPosition = "right",
    drawConnectors = TRUE,
    widthConnectors = 0.5,
    max.overlaps = 20
)
print(volcano_AD_vs_Old)
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\AD_vs_Old_volcano.png")
```

### Interpretation — AD vs Old
This is our most biologically important comparison — it reveals genes 
that change specifically in AD beyond normal aging. The plot is 
moderately dense with 684 total DEGs and Y axis reaching ~12.

**Top downregulated genes:**
- **SST** — Somatostatin, appears again confirming its loss is 
  AD-specific not aging-related. SST-expressing interneurons are 
  among the first neurons lost in AD
- **VGF** — Nerve growth factor inducible, supports neuronal survival 
  and synaptic plasticity. Reduced VGF is directly linked to cognitive 
  decline in AD
- **CRH** — Corticotropin releasing hormone, stress axis neuropeptide. 
  Loss disrupts HPA axis regulation in AD
- **SELE** — Selectin E, vascular adhesion molecule indicating vascular 
  component of AD pathology
- **MPO** — Myeloperoxidase, marker of microglial activation and 
  neuroinflammation
- **CTSG** — Cathepsin G, most extremely downregulated gene (log2FC ~-6). 
  Neutrophil serine protease involved in neuroinflammation

**Top upregulated genes:**
- **TFCP2L1** — Transcription factor, aberrant stem cell pathway 
  activation in AD neurons
- **SGO1** — Shugoshin, chromosomal segregation protein suggesting 
  chromosomal instability in AD
- **VTRNA2-1** — Vault RNA, non-coding RNA involved in apoptosis

**Key biological insight:**
SST and VGF are the standout hits in this comparison. SST loss 
specifically in AD vs Old confirms this is disease-specific not aging. 
VGF loss represents reduced neurotrophic support — accelerating neuronal 
death in AD. Both have been proposed as CSF biomarkers for AD in multiple 
independent clinical studies.

**Overall conclusion:**
This volcano plot reveals the true molecular fingerprint of Alzheimer's 
Disease — distinct from normal aging. The dominant theme is loss of 
neuropeptides, neurotrophic support and normal inflammatory regulation, 
with aberrant activation of cell cycle and stress response pathways.

---

### Volcano Plot 3 — Old vs Young
```{r eval=FALSE}
volcano_Old_vs_Young <- EnhancedVolcano(res_Old_vs_Young_df,
    lab      = res_Old_vs_Young_df$gene,
    x        = "log2FoldChange",
    y        = "padj",
    title    = "Old vs Young",
    subtitle = "Total DEGs: 361 | Up: 56 | Down: 305",
    pCutoff  = 0.05,
    FCcutoff = 1.0,
    pointSize = 2.0,
    labSize  = 3.0,
    col      = c("grey70", "grey70", "steelblue", "#3498DB"),
    colAlpha = 0.6,
    legendPosition = "right",
    drawConnectors = TRUE,
    widthConnectors = 0.5,
    max.overlaps = 20
)
print(volcano_Old_vs_Young)
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\Old_vs_Young_volcano.png")
```

### Interpretation — Old vs Young
This plot looks strikingly different from both AD comparisons — and 
that difference is itself the biological message. The plot is sparse 
and compressed — most dots cluster near the bottom (low significance) 
and centre (small fold change).

**Y axis only reaches ~7** compared to ~16 for AD vs Young — aging 
changes are real but nowhere near as statistically powerful as AD.

**Directionality — 305 down vs only 56 up:**
Aging downregulates 5x more genes than it upregulates. This makes 
perfect biological sense — aging is characterized by declining cellular 
activity, reduced metabolic rate, loss of transcriptional plasticity 
and epigenetic silencing. The aging brain becomes quieter, not noisier.

**Top downregulated genes:**
- **CPS1** — Carbamoyl phosphate synthase, urea cycle enzyme. 
  Reduced metabolic activity with aging
- **FGFRL1** — FGF receptor, growth factor signaling declines with age
- **GPER1** — Estrogen receptor, hormonal signaling changes with aging
- **ALPL** — Alkaline phosphatase, reduced mineralization with aging

**Top upregulated genes:**
- **AIFM3** — Apoptosis inducing factor, mild increase in apoptotic 
  signaling with age
- **PYGM** — Glycogen phosphorylase, energy metabolism shift with aging

**Key biological insight:**
The sparse, compressed appearance of this plot compared to the AD 
comparisons is itself the most important finding — it visually 
demonstrates that AD is NOT simply accelerated aging. Where aging 
whispers with 361 subtle changes, Alzheimer's Disease shouts with 
3,929 dramatic disruptions.

**Overall conclusion:**
Normal brain aging causes subtle, predominantly downward shifts in 
gene expression — reduced metabolic activity, declining growth factor 
signaling and modest increases in apoptotic pathways. This contrasts 
sharply with the massive bidirectional dysregulation seen in AD.

---

### Cross-Comparison Summary

| Comparison | DEGs | Direction | Max significance | Key theme |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AD vs Young | 3,929 | Balanced | -log10p ~16 | Massive disruption |
| AD vs Old | 684 | Slightly down | -log10p ~12 | Disease fingerprint |
| Old vs Young | 361 | Strongly down | -log10p ~7 | Quiet decline |

**The three-comparison narrative:**
```
Old vs Young   → Aging whispers.   361 subtle changes.
AD vs Old      → Disease speaks.   684 specific changes.
AD vs Young    → AD screams.       3,929 dramatic changes.
```

---

## Step 8 — Heatmaps

### What is a Heatmap?
A heatmap shows expression levels of multiple genes across all 
samples simultaneously using color:
- 🔴 Red → expression above average (positive Z-score)
- 🔵 Blue → expression below average (negative Z-score)  
- ⬜ White → expression at average level (Z-score near 0)

**Z-score scaling** is applied per row (gene) — converting raw 
expression to standard deviations above or below each gene's own 
mean. This allows comparison of patterns across genes with very 
different absolute expression levels.

**Dendrograms** show hierarchical clustering:
- Top dendrogram → samples grouped by transcriptomic similarity
- Left dendrogram → genes grouped by expression pattern similarity

**The key question:** Do samples cluster by diagnosis, and do gene 
blocks show consistent patterns within groups?

### Data used for heatmaps
Heatmaps use VST-transformed counts (not raw counts, not TPM):
```r
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)
vsd_matrix <- assay(vsd)
```
Top 50 genes selected by lowest adjusted p-value from each comparison.
Saved for reproducibility: vsd_object.RData

---

### Heatmap 1 — Top 50 DEGs: AD vs Old
```{r eval=FALSE}
top50_AD_vs_Old <- res_AD_vs_Old_df %>%
    filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
    arrange(padj) %>%
    head(50) %>%
    pull(gene)

heatmap_data_2 <- vsd_matrix[top50_AD_vs_Old, ]
heatmap_data_2_scaled <- t(scale(t(heatmap_data_2)))

pheatmap(heatmap_data_2_scaled,
    annotation_col    = annotation_col,
    annotation_colors = annotation_colors,
    color = colorRampPalette(rev(brewer.pal(11, "RdBu")))(100),
    cluster_rows = TRUE, cluster_cols = TRUE,
    show_rownames = TRUE, show_colnames = FALSE,
    fontsize_row = 8,
    main = "Top 50 DEGs: AD vs Old",
    filename = paste0(results_dir, "heatmap_AD_vs_Old.png"),
    width = 10, height = 12)
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\heatmap_AD_vs_Old.png")
```

### Interpretation — AD vs Old Heatmap

**Column clustering:**
Samples split into two major clusters — a mixed AD/Old cluster and 
a predominantly Young cluster on the right. This confirms PCA 
findings that aged and AD brains share transcriptomic features, 
making them harder to separate than AD vs Young.

**Upregulated gene block (top — red in AD):**
- **TFCP2L1, TFDP2** → aberrant cell cycle re-entry in AD neurons
- **SGO1** → chromosomal instability
- **DNAH11** → axonal transport disruption
- **RAD52** → DNA damage response activation
- **PRKX** → signaling dysregulation

These genes collectively tell a story of **cellular stress** — AD 
neurons aberrantly activating cell division machinery, DNA repair 
and cytoskeletal remodeling.

**Downregulated gene block (bottom — blue in AD):**
- **SST** → deep blue in AD, confirming specific loss of somatostatin
- **VGF** → neurotrophic support lost in AD
- **CRH, ADCYAP1** → neuropeptide loss
- **ACO2** → mitochondrial dysfunction
- **TXN2** → oxidative stress
- **MPO, SELE** → neuroinflammation and vascular dysfunction

These genes tell a story of **neuronal identity loss** — progressive 
silencing of neuropeptides, metabolic enzymes and neurotrophic factors.

**Brain region effect:**
No clear separation by region in column clustering — confirms region 
is not a dominant source of variation and our covariate correction 
was appropriate.

**Overall conclusion:**
Two clear gene programs emerge in AD vs Old — aberrant activation 
(cell cycle, DNA damage, cytoskeletal) and neuronal identity loss 
(neuropeptides, neurotrophic factors, metabolic enzymes). SST and 
VGF stand out as most consistently lost — both known AD biomarkers, 
giving our analysis strong biological validation.

---

### Heatmap 2 — Top 50 DEGs: AD vs Young
```{r eval=FALSE}
top50_AD_vs_Young <- res_AD_vs_Young_df %>%
    filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
    arrange(padj) %>%
    head(50) %>%
    pull(gene)

heatmap_data_1 <- vsd_matrix[top50_AD_vs_Young, ]
heatmap_data_1_scaled <- t(scale(t(heatmap_data_1)))

pheatmap(heatmap_data_1_scaled,
    annotation_col    = annotation_col,
    annotation_colors = annotation_colors,
    color = colorRampPalette(rev(brewer.pal(11, "RdBu")))(100),
    cluster_rows = TRUE, cluster_cols = TRUE,
    show_rownames = TRUE, show_colnames = FALSE,
    fontsize_row = 8,
    main = "Top 50 DEGs: AD vs Young",
    filename = paste0(results_dir, "heatmap_AD_vs_Young.png"),
    width = 10, height = 12)
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics("\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\heatmap_AD_vs_Young.png")
```

### Interpretation — AD vs Young Heatmap

**Column clustering:**
Much cleaner separation than AD vs Old. AD samples cluster together 
on the left showing high expression of the top gene block. Old and 
Young samples cluster together on the right — confirming that in the 
context of 3,929 AD vs Young DEGs, Old and Young brains look more 
similar to each other than either does to AD.

**Downregulated gene block (top — blue in AD, red in Young/Old):**
- **ACTB** → Beta-actin loss indicates severe neurodegeneration
- **POLR2A** → RNA Polymerase II reduced — transcriptional shutdown
- **BECN1** → Autophagy master regulator. Its loss allows Tau and 
  Amyloid-beta accumulation — directly relevant to AD pathology
- **VCP** → Protein quality control — proteostasis failure in AD
- **SST** → Somatostatin, consistently lost across both heatmaps
- **RBM3** → Neuroprotective RNA binding protein
- **PHB2** → Mitochondrial protein quality control
- **EIF6** → Translation initiation — reduced protein synthesis
- **GSS** → Glutathione synthetase — antioxidant defense failure

> BECN1 downregulation is particularly significant — autophagy 
> failure directly causes protein aggregate accumulation, the 
> hallmark pathology of Alzheimer's Disease.

**Upregulated gene block (bottom — red in AD, blue in Young/Old):**
- **RASSF3** → Tumor suppressor, apoptosis regulation
- **FOXJ1** → Transcription factor, ciliary function
- **DDIT4L** → DNA damage stress response
- **DNAH11** → Dynein axonal transport — appears in BOTH heatmaps!
- **CREBBP** → CREB binding protein — memory and synaptic plasticity 
  dysregulation directly connects to AD cognitive symptoms
- **RNU11** → Splicing regulation
- **STK38** → Cell survival signaling

**Genes appearing in BOTH heatmaps — the core AD signature:**
These genes are consistently dysregulated regardless of which control 
group is used — making them the most robust AD-associated genes in 
this dataset:

| Gene | Direction | Biological Role |
|---|---|---|
| SST | Down | Neuropeptide biomarker |
| DNAH11 | Up | Axonal transport |
| TXN2 | Down | Oxidative stress |
| ESYT1 | Down | Membrane contact sites |
| MED29 | Down | Transcriptional regulation |
| RABEPK | Down | Vesicle trafficking |
| DPH2 | Down | Diphthamide biosynthesis |
| DHRS7B | Down | Metabolic enzyme |
| HINT2 | Down | Nucleotide binding |

These overlapping genes represent the primary candidates for 
follow-up validation in future experiments.

**Overall conclusion:**
This heatmap shows the full scale of transcriptomic disruption in 
AD vs Young. Basic cellular machinery (ACTB, POLR2A), autophagy 
(BECN1), protein quality control (VCP) and antioxidant defense (GSS) 
are all lost — indicating widespread cellular dysfunction beyond 
targeted pathway effects. The clean separation of AD from both Old 
and Young confirms AD is a transcriptomically distinct disease state.

---

## Phase 3 — Complete Summary

### Files saved

| File | Location | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| `AD_vs_Young_results.csv` | results/DESeq2/ | Full DESeq2 results |
| `AD_vs_Old_results.csv` | results/DESeq2/ | Full DESeq2 results |
| `Old_vs_Young_results.csv` | results/DESeq2/ | Full DESeq2 results |
| `dds_object.RData` | results/DESeq2/ | DESeq2 + results objects |
| `vsd_object.RData` | results/DESeq2/ | VST transformed counts |
| `PCA_plot.png` | results/DESeq2/ | Sample clustering plot |
| `AD_vs_Young_volcano.png` | results/DESeq2/ | Volcano plot |
| `AD_vs_Old_volcano.png` | results/DESeq2/ | Volcano plot |
| `Old_vs_Young_volcano.png` | results/DESeq2/ | Volcano plot |
| `heatmap_AD_vs_Young.png` | results/DESeq2/ | Top 50 DEG heatmap |
| `heatmap_AD_vs_Old.png` | results/DESeq2/ | Top 50 DEG heatmap |

### Key biological findings from Phase 3

**Finding 1 — Scale of dysregulation**
AD causes ~10x more transcriptomic changes than normal aging (3,929 
vs 361 DEGs) — confirming AD is a distinct pathological process, 
not accelerated aging.

**Finding 2 — Disease progression axis**
PCA PC1 explains 59% of all variation and represents a Young → Old 
→ AD progression axis — the transcriptome shifts gradually with 
aging and dramatically with disease.

**Finding 3 — Independent biomarker rediscovery**
SST (Somatostatin) and VGF were independently identified as top 
downregulated genes in AD — both are established AD biomarkers in 
published literature. This validates our entire analytical pipeline.

**Finding 4 — Core AD signature**
9 genes are consistently dysregulated in AD across both control 
comparisons — SST, DNAH11, TXN2, ESYT1, MED29, RABEPK, DPH2, 
DHRS7B and HINT2. These represent the most robust AD transcriptomic 
signatures in this dataset.

**Finding 5 — Biological themes**
AD is characterized by simultaneous loss of neuronal identity 
(neuropeptides, neurotrophic factors) and aberrant activation of 
stress responses (DNA damage, cell cycle re-entry, apoptosis).

**Next:** Phase 4 — Functional Enrichment Analysis to identify 
which biological pathways and gene sets are systematically 
dysregulated in AD.


---

# Phase 4 — Functional Enrichment Analysis

## Overview
In Phase 3 we identified hundreds to thousands of differentially expressed 
genes. But a list of gene names alone is hard to interpret biologically. 
Functional enrichment analysis converts gene lists into biological stories 
by asking:

> *"Do these DEGs belong to specific biological pathways, processes or 
> functions more than we would expect by chance?"*

For example instead of saying:
> *"BECN1, ATG5, ATG7, ATG12 are all downregulated"*

Enrichment analysis says:
> *"The AUTOPHAGY pathway is significantly downregulated in AD"*

---

## Two Complementary Approaches

### Approach 1 — Over Representation Analysis (ORA)
- Takes a list of **significant DEGs only**
- Asks: are any pathways enriched in this list vs the background genome?
- Tool: `clusterProfiler`
- Limitation: requires enough significant genes to reach statistical power

### Approach 2 — Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA)
- Uses **ALL genes ranked by fold change** — not just significant ones
- Asks: do genes in a pathway tend to be consistently up or down?
- Tool: `fgsea`
- More powerful — detects subtle but consistent pathway-level shifts
- Tells you DIRECTION — is the pathway going up or down in AD?

**We run both** — they complement each other perfectly. ORA gives 
clear significant pathway hits when enough DEGs exist. GSEA detects 
pathway signals even when individual gene changes are subtle.

---

## Databases Used

| Database | Contents | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| GO Biological Process | Biological processes (autophagy, apoptosis) | clusterProfiler |
| GO Molecular Function | Molecular activities (kinase activity, binding) | clusterProfiler |
| GO Cellular Component | Cellular locations (mitochondria, proteasome) | clusterProfiler |
| KEGG | Curated biological pathways | clusterProfiler |
| MSigDB Hallmarks | 50 well-defined biological states | fgsea |
| MSigDB KEGG Legacy | 186 KEGG pathway gene sets | fgsea |

---

## Script information
All ORA code: `04_enrichment_analysis.R`
All GSEA code: `05_GSEA_analysis.R`

---

## Part A — Over Representation Analysis (ORA)

### Step 1 — Install and load packages
```{r eval=FALSE}
BiocManager::install(c(
    "clusterProfiler",
    "org.Hs.eg.db",
    "enrichplot",
    "fgsea"
))
BiocManager::install("msigdbr")
install.packages("ggupset")
```
```{r}
library(clusterProfiler)
library(org.Hs.eg.db)
library(enrichplot)
library(fgsea)
library(msigdbr)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

results_dir <- "\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\DESeq2\\"
enrich_dir  <- "\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\maulibhavsar\\AD_RNAseq_Project\\results\\enrichment\\"

res_AD_vs_Young_df  <- read.csv(paste0(results_dir, "AD_vs_Young_results.csv"))
res_AD_vs_Old_df    <- read.csv(paste0(results_dir, "AD_vs_Old_results.csv"))

cat("Results loaded!\n")
```

---

### Step 2 — Prepare gene lists for ORA

ORA requires two inputs:
1. **Significant DEG list** — genes that passed our statistical thresholds
2. **Background universe** — all genes that were tested in DESeq2
```{r eval=FALSE}
# AD vs Young — our most powerful comparison for ORA
sig_AD_vs_Young <- res_AD_vs_Young_df %>%
    filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
    filter(padj < 0.05) %>%
    filter(abs(log2FoldChange) > 0.5)

# Convert gene symbols to Entrez IDs
# Required by most enrichment databases
sig_entrez_Young <- bitr(
    sig_AD_vs_Young$gene,
    fromType = "SYMBOL",
    toType   = "ENTREZID",
    OrgDb    = org.Hs.eg.db
)

# Background universe — all genes tested in DESeq2
universe_Young <- bitr(
    res_AD_vs_Young_df$gene,
    fromType = "SYMBOL",
    toType   = "ENTREZID",
    OrgDb    = org.Hs.eg.db
)

cat("Significant DEGs:", nrow(sig_AD_vs_Young), "\n")
cat("Converted to Entrez:", nrow(sig_entrez_Young), "\n")
cat("Universe size:", nrow(universe_Young), "\n")
```

**Results:**
- AD vs Young significant DEGs: 2,912
- Converted to Entrez: 2,649 (9% lost — non-coding RNAs and pseudogenes)
- Universe size: 21,316 genes

> 💡 **Why do we lose some genes in conversion?**
> Some genes are non-coding RNAs (RNU11, SNORA44), novel uncharacterized
> genes (LOC codes) or pseudogenes that have no Entrez ID. This is
> completely normal — typically 5-10% of genes are lost.

> 💡 **Why use AD vs Young for ORA?**
> ORA statistical power depends on number of input genes. AD vs Old
> only had 436 converted genes — not enough power. AD vs Young with
> 2,649 genes provides sufficient statistical power to detect enriched
> pathways after multiple testing correction.

---

### Step 3 — GO Biological Process Enrichment
```{r eval=FALSE}
go_BP <- enrichGO(
    gene          = sig_entrez_Young$ENTREZID,
    universe      = universe_Young$ENTREZID,
    OrgDb         = org.Hs.eg.db,
    ont           = "BP",
    pAdjustMethod = "BH",
    pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
    qvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
    readable      = TRUE
)

cat("GO BP terms found:", nrow(go_BP), "\n")
```

**Parameters explained:**
- `ont = "BP"` → Biological Process ontology
- `pAdjustMethod = "BH"` → Benjamini-Hochberg multiple testing correction
- `pvalueCutoff = 0.05` → only return significant terms
- `readable = TRUE` → show gene symbols instead of Entrez IDs

**Result: 23 significant GO BP terms**

#### GO BP Dotplot
```{r eval=FALSE}
dotplot_BP <- dotplot(go_BP,
    showCategory = 15,
    title = "GO Biological Process — AD vs Young",
    font.size = 10
) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
print(dotplot_BP)
```
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(enrich_dir, "GO_BP_dotplot.png"))
```

#### GO BP Barplot
```{r eval=FALSE}
barplot_BP <- barplot(go_BP,
    showCategory = 15,
    title = "GO Biological Process — AD vs Young",
    font.size = 10
) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
print(barplot_BP)
```
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(enrich_dir, "GO_BP_barplot.png"))
```

#### GO BP Interpretation

**Overall theme: Energy Metabolism and Mitochondrial Dysfunction**

Every single enriched term relates to one central biological theme —
mitochondrial energy metabolism:

| Term | Count | Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Generation of precursor metabolites and energy | 92 | Master energy production process failing |
| Mitochondrion organization | 85 | Mitochondrial structure disrupted |
| Nucleotide metabolic process | 88 | ATP building blocks affected |
| ATP metabolic process | 48 | Direct ATP production failure |
| Energy derivation by oxidation | 67 | Oxidative phosphorylation disrupted |
| Aerobic respiration | 38 | Oxygen utilization failing |
| Cellular respiration | 49 | Respiratory chain dysfunction |

**Why is energy metabolism the top finding?**
The brain is the most metabolically demanding organ — consuming 20% of
total body energy despite being only 2% of body weight. Neurons depend
almost entirely on mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation for ATP. In AD:
- Amyloid-beta oligomers directly damage mitochondrial membranes
- Tau tangles disrupt mitochondrial transport along axons
- ATP production drops — neurons cannot maintain ion gradients or
  synaptic transmission
- Energy failure accelerates neuronal death

Our analysis independently rediscovered this using only gene expression
data with no prior assumptions — providing strong biological validation.

---

### Step 4 — GO Cellular Component Enrichment
```{r eval=FALSE}
go_CC <- enrichGO(
    gene          = sig_entrez_Young$ENTREZID,
    universe      = universe_Young$ENTREZID,
    OrgDb         = org.Hs.eg.db,
    ont           = "CC",
    pAdjustMethod = "BH",
    pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
    qvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
    readable      = TRUE
)

cat("GO CC terms found:", nrow(go_CC), "\n")
```

**Result: 21 significant GO CC terms**

GO Molecular Function (MF) returned 0 significant terms for both
comparisons — documented as underpowered for MF enrichment with
this dataset.

#### GO CC Dotplot
```{r eval=FALSE}
dotplot_CC <- dotplot(go_CC,
    showCategory = 15,
    title = "GO Cellular Component — AD vs Young",
    font.size = 10
) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
print(dotplot_CC)
```
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(enrich_dir, "GO_CC_dotplot.png"))
```

#### GO CC Interpretation

**Overall theme: Proteasome Complex and Vesicular Transport**

| Term | p.adjust | Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Proteasome complex | 1.9e-08 | Most significant! Protein degradation machinery |
| Endopeptidase complex | 1.1e-06 | Protein cleavage complexes |
| Proteasome accessory complex | 2.2e-05 | Proteasome regulation |
| Proteasome core complex | 4.1e-05 | Core degradation machinery |
| Vesicle lumen | 9.3e-05 | Vesicular transport affected |
| Secretory granule lumen | 1.5e-04 | Secretion machinery disrupted |
| Mitochondrial inner membrane | 1.3e-03 | Confirms mitochondrial dysfunction |

**Proteasome enrichment is a landmark finding!**
The proteasome is the cell's main protein disposal system. When it
fails in AD neurons, misfolded Tau and Amyloid-beta ACCUMULATE instead
of being cleared. Combined with our earlier BECN1 (autophagy) finding
from the heatmap, we now see TWO protein clearance systems failing
simultaneously in AD:
1. **Proteasome** — direct protein degradation (GO CC finding)
2. **Autophagy** — bulk protein clearance (BECN1 heatmap finding)

This double failure of protein quality control directly explains the
accumulation of protein aggregates that define AD pathology.

---

### Step 5 — KEGG Pathway Analysis
```{r eval=FALSE}
kegg_Young <- enrichKEGG(
    gene          = sig_entrez_Young$ENTREZID,
    organism      = "hsa",
    pAdjustMethod = "BH",
    pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
    qvalueCutoff  = 0.05
)

cat("KEGG pathways found:", nrow(kegg_Young), "\n")
```

**Result: 22 significant KEGG pathways**

AD vs Old returned 0 KEGG pathways — documented as underpowered
for ORA with 436 genes. GSEA was used instead for AD vs Old.

#### KEGG Dotplot
```{r eval=FALSE}
dotplot_kegg <- dotplot(kegg_Young,
    showCategory = 15,
    title = "KEGG Pathways — AD vs Young",
    font.size = 10
) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"))
print(dotplot_kegg)
```
```{r echo=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0(enrich_dir, "KEGG_dotplot_AD_vs_Young.png"))
```

#### KEGG Interpretation

**Top 15 KEGG pathways:**

| Pathway | GeneRatio | p.adjust | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Proteasome | 26/1005 | 1.06e-11 | 🔴 Most significant |
| Huntington disease | 75/1005 | 5.70e-10 | 🔴 |
| **Alzheimer disease** | 87/1005 | 8.58e-10 | 🔴 **KEY FINDING** |
| Parkinson disease | 66/1005 | 3.55e-09 | 🔴 |
| ALS | 77/1005 | 2.62e-07 | 🔴 |
| Prion disease | 61/1005 | 1.02e-06 | 🔴 |
| Neurodegeneration — multiple | 91/1005 | 1.18e-06 | 🔴 |
| TCA cycle | 12/1005 | 9.38e-04 | ✅ |
| Carbon metabolism | 28/1005 | 9.20e-04 | ✅ |
| DNA replication | 13/1005 | 1.44e-03 | ✅ |
| Cell cycle | 32/1005 | 6.30e-03 | ✅ |

**The Alzheimer's Disease KEGG pathway is directly enriched!**
87 of our DEGs map directly to the KEGG Alzheimer's Disease pathway
with padj = 8.58e-10. This is the ultimate biological validation —
starting from raw RNA-seq counts with zero prior assumptions, our
analysis independently rediscovered the Alzheimer's Disease pathway.

**Multiple neurodegenerative diseases enriched simultaneously:**
AD, Parkinson's, Huntington's, ALS and Prion disease all enriched.
This does NOT mean patients have all these diseases. These pathways
SHARE molecular mechanisms:
- Mitochondrial dysfunction
- Proteasome failure
- Oxidative stress
- Protein aggregation
These are universal neurodegeneration mechanisms our DEGs collectively
capture.

**ORA Summary for AD vs Old:**
All GO and KEGG ORA analyses returned 0 significant results for AD
vs Old due to insufficient statistical power (436 genes). This is
documented as a known limitation of ORA with small gene lists.
GSEA was used as the primary method for AD vs Old analysis.

---

## Part B — Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA)

### What is GSEA?

ORA asks: *"Are pathway genes overrepresented in my significant DEG list?"*

GSEA asks something more powerful:
*"If I rank ALL genes from most upregulated to most downregulated,
do genes belonging to a pathway tend to cluster at the top or bottom?"*
```
Most upregulated ←————————————→ Most downregulated
gene1  gene2  gene3 ... gene21000
  ↑                         ↑
Pathway genes               Pathway genes
here = UPREGULATED          here = DOWNREGULATED
```

**Key advantages over ORA:**
- Uses ALL 21,000 genes — not just significant ones
- Detects subtle but consistent pathway-level shifts
- Gives DIRECTION — pathway going up or down in AD
- Much more powerful for smaller gene lists like AD vs Old

### Step 1 — Prepare gene sets and ranked lists
```{r eval=FALSE}
# Get MSigDB gene sets
hallmarks <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens",
                     collection = "H") %>%
    dplyr::select(gs_name, ncbi_gene) %>%
    mutate(ncbi_gene = as.character(ncbi_gene))

kegg_sets <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens",
                     collection = "C2",
                     subcollection = "CP:KEGG_LEGACY") %>%
    dplyr::select(gs_name, ncbi_gene) %>%
    mutate(ncbi_gene = as.character(ncbi_gene))

# Convert to list format required by fgsea
hallmarks_list <- split(hallmarks$ncbi_gene, hallmarks$gs_name)
kegg_list      <- split(kegg_sets$ncbi_gene, kegg_sets$gs_name)

cat("Hallmark gene sets:", length(unique(hallmarks$gs_name)), "\n")
cat("KEGG gene sets:", length(unique(kegg_sets$gs_name)), "\n")
# Hallmark: 50, KEGG: 186
```
```{r eval=FALSE}
# Prepare ranked gene lists — ALL genes ranked by log2FC
# AD vs Young
ranked_Young_entrez <- bitr(
    res_AD_vs_Young_df %>%
        filter(!is.na(log2FoldChange), !is.na(padj)) %>%
        pull(gene),
    fromType = "SYMBOL",
    toType   = "ENTREZID",
    OrgDb    = org.Hs.eg.db
) %>%
    left_join(
        res_AD_vs_Young_df %>%
            dplyr::select(gene, log2FoldChange),
        by = c("SYMBOL" = "gene")
    ) %>%
    arrange(desc(log2FoldChange))

ranks_Young <- ranked_Young_entrez$log2FoldChange
names(ranks_Young) <- ranked_Young_entrez$ENTREZID

# AD vs Old
ranked_Old_entrez <- bitr(
    res_AD_vs_Old_df %>%
        filter(!is.na(log2FoldChange), !is.na(padj)) %>%
        pull(gene),
    fromType = "SYMBOL",
    toType   = "ENTREZID",
    OrgDb    = org.Hs.eg.db
) %>%
    left_join(
        res_AD_vs_Old_df %>%
            dplyr::select(gene, log2FoldChange),
        by = c("SYMBOL" = "gene")
    ) %>%
    arrange(desc(log2FoldChange))

ranks_Old <- ranked_Old_entrez$log2FoldChange
names(ranks_Old) <- ranked_Old_entrez$ENTREZID

cat("Ranked genes AD vs Young:", length(ranks_Young), "\n")
cat("Ranked genes AD vs Old:", length(ranks_Old), "\n")
```

---

### Step 2 — Run GSEA
```{r eval=FALSE}
# set.seed(42) ensures reproducibility
# GSEA uses random permutations internally
# Same seed = identical results every run

set.seed(42)
gsea_hallmarks_Young <- fgsea(pathways = hallmarks_list,
                               stats = ranks_Young,
                               minSize = 15, maxSize = 500)

set.seed(42)
gsea_hallmarks_Old <- fgsea(pathways = hallmarks_list,
                             stats = ranks_Old,
                             minSize = 15, maxSize = 500)

set.seed(42)
gsea_kegg_Young <- fgsea(pathways = kegg_list,
                          stats = ranks_Young,
                          minSize = 15, maxSize = 500)

set.seed(42)
gsea_kegg_Old <- fgsea(pathways = kegg_list,
                        stats = ranks_Old,
                        minSize = 15, maxSize = 500)
```

**Parameters explained:**
- `minSize = 15` → only test pathways with at least 15 genes
- `maxSize = 500` → exclude pathways with >500 genes (too generic)
- `set.seed(42)` → reproducibility

**GSEA Results Summary:**

| Analysis | Significant pathways |
|---|---|
| Hallmarks — AD vs Young | **29** |
| Hallmarks — AD vs Old | **14** |
| KEGG — AD vs Young | **33** |
| KEGG — AD vs Old | **30** |

> 💡 **The power of GSEA demonstrated:** ORA found 0 pathways for
> AD vs Old. GSEA found 44 significant pathways (14 + 30) for the
> same comparison. This is because GSEA uses all 19,000 ranked genes
